question: How do generics, variance, and `out`/`in` modifiers work in Kotlin?
answer:
Generics give type-safe code that works across types. Kotlin treats generics with declaration-site variance (Scala-style):

• Invariant (default) — `Box<T>` is unrelated to `Box<Subtype>` even when T <: Subtype.
• `out T` — covariant. `Producer<out T>` — produces T's only. `Producer<Dog>` is a `Producer<Animal>`.
• `in T` — contravariant. `Consumer<in T>` — consumes T's only. `Consumer<Animal>` is a `Consumer<Dog>`.

Use-site variance with `Box<out T>` / `Box<in T>` exists too (akin to Java wildcards).

Reified generics (`inline fun <reified T>`) preserve T at runtime — enables `T::class` checks without `Class<T>` parameter.

question: What are inline functions and reified type parameters in Kotlin?
answer:
An `inline fun` is replaced at compile time with the body of the function copied into the call site. The lambda parameters are inlined too — no Function<...> object allocation, no extra invocation.

Why:
• Performance — avoid lambda allocation in hot loops.
• Non-local return — `return` inside an inline lambda returns from the ENCLOSING function.
• Reified generics — when a type parameter is `reified`, the compiler knows T at the call site and you can write `T::class`, `is T`, `as T`.

Downsides:
• Code bloat if overused — every call site copies the body.
• `noinline` opts out of inlining for specific lambda parameters; `crossinline` forbids non-local returns from a specific lambda.

question: What are property delegates? Explain `by lazy`, `by Delegates.observable`, custom delegates.
answer:
Property delegation lets a property's get/set be implemented by another object — the `by` keyword forwards reads/writes to that object's `getValue` / `setValue`.

Built-in delegates:
• `lazy { ... }` — value computed on first read; thread-safe by default.
• `Delegates.observable(initial) { prop, old, new -> ... }` — invoke a callback on every set.
• `Delegates.vetoable(initial) { ... }` — same, but the lambda can reject the change.
• `notNull()` — required-by-runtime substitute for `lateinit` for primitive types.
• `by map` — back a property with a Map (great for dynamic schemas, deserialization).

Custom delegates are easy: implement `getValue` / `setValue` operators (or use `ReadOnlyProperty` / `ReadWriteProperty`).

question: How do you write a type-safe DSL in Kotlin?
answer:
Kotlin DSLs leverage three features:

• Lambdas with receiver: `block: A.() -> Unit` — inside the lambda `this` is `A`, members of A are in scope.
• Extension functions — extend a type for the duration of a builder.
• `@DslMarker` annotation — restrict which receivers are visible inside nested builders, preventing accidental cross-scope calls.

Typical pattern: a top-level builder function takes a lambda with receiver, instantiates a builder, runs the lambda, returns the constructed result.

Famous DSLs in the wild: Gradle Kotlin DSL, kotlinx.html, Compose, Ktor routing, Exposed SQL DSL.

question: What are typealiases and when should you use them?
answer:
`typealias` introduces a name for an existing type. It does NOT create a new type — it's purely a compile-time alias the compiler resolves to the underlying type.

Good uses:
• Shorten complex generic types: `typealias Cache = Map<String, ByteArray>`.
• Clarify intent: `typealias UserId = Long` reads better than passing raw Long around.
• Function-type aliases: `typealias Validator<T> = (T) -> Result<Unit>`.
• Deprecate old names while moving toward new ones.

Limitations:
• Not a wrapper — `UserId` and `Long` are the SAME type. The compiler does not enforce that you don't mix them.
• For real type-safety use a value class (inline class) or a wrapper data class.

question: What are value classes (inline classes) in Kotlin?
answer:
A value class is a wrapper around a single value that the compiler can ELIDE at runtime — replacing every use of the wrapper with the underlying value. You get type-safety at the language level and no allocation overhead at the JVM/binary level (in most cases).

• Declared with `@JvmInline value class`.
• Wraps exactly one property.
• Compiler still allocates when you use the wrapper as a generic / nullable / interface type — be aware.

Idiomatic uses: domain primitives (UserId, Email, Money), units (Px, Dp, Hz), bitfields.

Replaces the older `inline class` keyword (still works in some Kotlin versions).
